supply_chain.dependencies: Single-source suppliers for application-specific integrated circuits used in transmitters, seals used for applicators, and certain polymers used to synthesize polymeric membranes for sensors. Reliance on third-party suppliers for components and materials. Custom nature of parts makes it difficult to quickly establish alternative or replacement suppliers.
supply_chain.risks: Supply disruptions from single-source suppliers. Manufacturing difficulties at facilities could adversely affect operations. Dependence on third-party suppliers makes company vulnerable to supply disruptions, suboptimal quality, non-compliance and price fluctuations. Suppliers may fail to comply with protocols, regulations, or experience equipment malfunction, environmental factors, cyber-attacks, public health emergencies. Difficulty in qualifying alternative suppliers for single-source supplies. Suppliers may discontinue production of critical components. Recent manufacturing and inventory challenges for G7 have resulted in disruptions in ability to supply certain markets including U.S. and other countries.

regulatory_risks: FDA regulatory compliance and approval requirements for medical devices
Failure to comply with FDA Quality System Regulation (QSR) and Good Manufacturing Practices
Medical Device Reporting (MDR) regulations requiring incident reporting
Risk of product recalls, seizures, or withdrawal from market due to regulatory non-compliance
Changes to FDA approval standards and processes, including cybersecurity requirements
International regulatory requirements varying by country (CE Mark in EU, country-specific approvals)
European Union Medical Device Regulation (MDR) compliance requirements
Brexit impact on regulatory approvals in UK vs EU
Anti-Kickback Statute and federal False Claims Act compliance
Stark Law (Federal Physician Self-Referral Law) compliance
Civil Monetary Penalties Law (CMPL) including Beneficiary Inducement provisions
HIPAA Privacy, Security, and Breach Notification Rules compliance
State data breach notification laws
Federal Trade Commission (FTC) enforcement on consumer protection and data practices
California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) and California Privacy Rights Act (CPRA)
Washington My Health My Data Act
Multiple state privacy laws (Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut, Utah, Oregon, Texas, Florida, Montana, Delaware, Nebraska, New Hampshire, Iowa, Tennessee, Indiana)
EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) with fines up to 20 million euros or 4% of global turnover
UK Data Protection Act and UK GDPR
Data localization laws in various countries
EU Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD)
California climate-related reporting requirements (greenhouse gas emissions, climate risks)
SEC cybersecurity risk management and disclosure rules
Physician Payment Sunshine Act reporting requirements
State marketing and transparency laws for device manufacturers
U.S. Foreign Corrupt Practices Act (FCPA) compliance
UK Bribery Act compliance
Export control regulations (U.S. Department of Treasury Office of Foreign Assets Control, Bureau of Industry and Security)
Medicare and Medicaid coverage and reimbursement policy changes
CMS Local Coverage Determination (LCD) requirements
Affordable Care Act (ACA) compliance requirements
Potential changes in healthcare policy under new presidential administration
OIG Special Fraud Alert on manufacturer Speaker Programs
AdvaMed Code of Ethics revisions
Food and Drug Omnibus Reform Act (FDORA) clinical trial diversity requirements
Environmental, health and safety laws governing hazardous materials handling
EU due diligence reporting requirements
Responsible Business Alliance sustainability standards
OECD BEPS (Base Erosion and Profit Shifting) tax framework
OECD Pillar Two global minimum tax (15%) implementation
California S.B. 167 and S.B. 175 (NOL suspension and R&D credit limitations)
Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP) requirements
Cybersecurity Maturity Model Certification (CMMC) requirements
AI regulations at federal, state and international levels (60 AI-related regulations in U.S. as of 2024)
EU AI Act (effective August 1, 2024)
HHS AI Strategic Plan (FAVES framework)

sanction_risks: U.S. economic sanctions administered by Office of Foreign Assets Control restricting business with certain countries, governments, entities and individuals
Potential violations of export control regulations could result in fines, penalties, denial of export privileges, asset seizures, debarment from government contracts
Fluctuations in trade policy and tariff regulations, including potential higher tariffs on imported goods and renegotiation of free trade agreements
Potential retaliatory tariffs imposed by foreign countries against U.S. goods
Restrictions on technology transfer and data flows to certain jurisdictions
